section.title: A Call for Justice, Not Empty Fasting
section.sectionRationale: Two years after the night visions, a delegation asks whether they should keep fasting over the fall of Jerusalem. God answers with a searching question: was your fasting ever really for Me? He recalls the earlier prophets who called for justice and mercy, and the hard hearts that ignored them and were scattered. Ritual without a heart for justice is worthless.
section.thesis: God does not want religious ritual performed for ourselves; He wants true justice, mercy, and compassion flowing from a soft heart, for the earlier generation's refusal to do justice, not their neglect of fasting, is what brought the exile.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the delegation's question about fasting; God's probing of self-centered ritual and His summons to the words of the earlier prophets; and the ancient call to true justice, met by hard hearts, resulting in the scattering that made the pleasant land desolate.

verses.text: In the fourth year of King Darius, the word of the LORD came to Zechariah on the fourth day of the ninth month, the month of Chislev.
Now the people of Bethel had sent Sharezer and Regem-melech, along with their men, to plead before the LORD
by asking the priests of the house of the LORD of Hosts, as well as the prophets, “Should I weep and {{tsum|fast}} in the fifth month, as I have done these many years?”
Then the word of the LORD of Hosts came to me, saying,
“Ask all the people of the land and the priests, ‘When you fasted and mourned in the fifth and seventh months for these seventy years, was it really for Me that you fasted?
And when you were eating and drinking, were you not doing so simply for yourselves?
Are these not the words that the LORD proclaimed through the earlier prophets, when Jerusalem and its surrounding towns were populous and prosperous, and the Negev and the foothills were inhabited?’”
Then the word of the LORD came to Zechariah, saying,
“This is what the LORD of Hosts says: ‘Administer true {{mishpat|justice}}. Show {{chesed|loving devotion}} and compassion to one another.
Do not oppress the widow or the fatherless, the foreigner or the poor. And do not plot evil in your hearts against one another.’
But they refused to pay attention and turned a stubborn shoulder; they stopped up their ears from hearing.

They made their hearts like flint and would not listen to the law or to the words that the LORD of Hosts had sent by His Spirit through the earlier prophets. Therefore great anger came from the LORD of Hosts.
And just as I had called and they would not listen, so when they called I would not listen, says the LORD of Hosts.

But I scattered them with a whirlwind among all the nations that they had not known, and the land was left desolate behind them so that no one could come or go. Thus they turned the pleasant land into a desolation.”

tsum.gloss: To fast, to abstain from food as an act of mourning, repentance, or seeking God. The people had fasted for seventy years to mourn the temple's destruction. God's question is not whether fasting is good but whether their fasting was ever truly directed to Him or was really a self-focused ritual, exposing the difference between outward observance and a heart turned to God.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of society according to God's standard, especially the fair treatment of the vulnerable. True mishpat is not merely correct legal procedure but active, faithful dealing that protects the weak. God summarizes the message of the earlier prophets as a call to render true justice, the very thing the exiles' ancestors refused to do.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast love, covenant kindness and mercy. It is the loyal, generous love God shows His people and calls them to show one another. Paired with compassion, it moves beyond bare justice to warm-hearted care for the neighbor, the widow, the orphan, the foreigner, and the poor, the fruit God desires far more than ritual.

basic.context: Two years after the night visions, a delegation from Bethel comes to ask the priests and prophets whether they should keep fasting in the fifth month to mourn Jerusalem's fall. God answers with a question of His own: in all those seventy years of fasting, was it really for Me, or for yourselves? He then recalls the message of the earlier prophets, a call to true justice and mercy that the pre-exile generation refused, which is why God scattered them and left the land desolate.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic teaching in response to a real question. God does not simply say yes or no about fasting; He redirects the whole issue to the heart. The point is timeless: religious ritual done for our own sake, without justice and mercy, is worthless to God. The history of the hard-hearted ancestors is a warning to the returned community not to repeat their sin.
basic.originalLanguages: Their practice was the tsum, the fast of mourning, kept for seventy years. God's real desire is mishpat, true justice, the right treatment of the vulnerable, joined with chesed, loving devotion, covenant kindness and mercy to one another. The earlier generation refused these, making their hearts like flint, and so were scattered.
basic.typology: The prophetic call to justice over empty ritual runs straight to Jesus, who quoted the prophets, I desire mercy and not sacrifice, and who denounced religion that neglects justice, mercy, and faithfulness. The hard heart of flint anticipates God's promise of a new heart, fulfilled by the Spirit in the new covenant.
basic.christ: Jesus stands squarely in this prophetic tradition. He rebuked those who tithed their spices while neglecting justice, mercy, and faithfulness, the weightier matters of the law. He is the one who perfectly loves justice and shows mercy, and who by His Spirit replaces our hearts of stone with hearts of flesh, so that the justice and mercy God always wanted can finally flow from within.
basic.god: God searches the heart behind the ritual and is not fooled by outward religion. He cares intensely about how the weak are treated, the widow, the orphan, the foreigner, the poor. He is patient, sending prophet after prophet, but His anger is real, and hardened hearts bring judgment. Yet even His question invites the people to become what He always wanted them to be.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 58 exposes fasting without justice. Micah 6 asks what the LORD requires: justice, mercy, and humble walking. Matthew 23 rebuilds the weightier matters of the law. Ezekiel 36 promises a new heart of flesh in place of stone.
basic.oneStory: God redirects a question about fasting to the heart of true religion: justice and mercy from a soft heart. The hard hearts that ignored the prophets brought the exile; the call points forward to Christ, who fulfills mercy over sacrifice and gives His people new hearts by the Spirit, so that the justice God always desired can at last be lived.

context.literary: Chapters 7 and 8 form a pair that answers the fasting question, moving from rebuke to promise. Chapter 7 looks back at the sin that caused the exile; chapter 8 looks ahead to blessing and turns the fasts into feasts. This section, chapter 7, is dated and framed by the delegation's question, then answered in two prophetic words.
context.historical: The date is 518 BC, the fourth year of Darius, two years into the temple rebuilding. The fasts commemorated events of the fall of Jerusalem: the fifth month for the temple's burning, the seventh for the murder of Gedaliah. With the temple rising again, the people reasonably ask whether the mourning fasts should continue.
context.canonical: The passage joins the great prophetic critique of hollow ritual found in Amos, Hosea, Isaiah, and Micah, and it echoes the covenant curse of scattering from Deuteronomy 28. Its call for justice and mercy is taken up by Jesus and the apostles as the heart of true, undefiled religion.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To redirect the community from a self-focused question about ritual to the true concern of God, justice and mercy from a soft heart, using the hard-hearted ancestors as a warning, so that the returned people would not repeat the sin that brought the exile.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: God's summons to justice, mercy, and compassion is prescriptive and enduring; the historical account of the ancestors' refusal and scattering is descriptive and cautionary. The abiding truths are that God wants heart-obedience over ritual and that He defends the vulnerable.

debates.question: Is God condemning fasting itself?

views.label: No, He questions the motive, not the practice
views.case: God asks whether the fasting was for Him or for themselves; the problem is self-focused ritual, not fasting as such, and chapter 8 turns the fasts into joyful feasts.

views.label: He relativizes ritual under justice
views.case: God subordinates all ritual, including fasting, to the weightier matters of justice and mercy, so that ritual without them is worthless, though not forbidden.

debates.bearing: Either way the lesson is that God desires heart-obedience and mercy, and treats ritual as empty when it is divorced from them.

debates.question: Why recall the pre-exile generation in answering a fasting question?

views.label: A warning against repeating their sin
views.case: The returned community is tempted to prize ritual while neglecting justice, exactly the sin that hardened their ancestors and brought the exile, so God holds up their history as a mirror.

views.label: To reframe what the fasts were really about
views.case: The fasts mourned a judgment caused by injustice, not by neglected ceremony; recalling the cause reorients the people to the justice and mercy that alone please God.

debates.bearing: Both readings press the same point: the community must pursue justice and mercy now rather than repeat the hard-hearted formalism of the past.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the passage. The people ask about the tsum, the mourning fast they had kept for seventy years, and God probes whether it was ever truly for Him. Against hollow ritual He sets what He has always wanted: mishpat, true justice, the right and faithful treatment of the vulnerable, not mere legal correctness but active protection of the widow, the orphan, the foreigner, and the poor; and chesed, loving devotion, the loyal covenant kindness and mercy that God shows His people and calls them to show one another. The tragedy is that the earlier generation heard this same summons and made their hearts like flint, refusing to listen, so that the God who called and was ignored, in turn would not listen when they called, and scattered them with a whirlwind. Ritual they had; mishpat and chesed they refused.

typology.type: The prophetic call of justice over ritual
typology.fulfillment: Jesus quoting I desire mercy and not sacrifice, and naming justice, mercy, and faithfulness the weightier matters of the law, Matthew 9:13 and 23:23.
The new heart of flesh God gives by His Spirit in the new covenant, Ezekiel 36:26-27 and 2 Corinthians 3:3.

typology.type: God's care for the widow, orphan, foreigner, and poor
typology.fulfillment: The pure and undefiled religion of caring for the vulnerable and the mercy embodied in Christ, James 1:27 and Luke 4:18.

study.christ: This passage stands in the great prophetic stream that flows directly to Jesus. When the Pharisees prized their scrupulous ritual, He answered them with the very heart of Zechariah 7: Go and learn what this means, I desire mercy and not sacrifice; and again, you tithe your mint and dill but have neglected the weightier matters of the law, justice and mercy and faithfulness. Jesus is the one who perfectly loves justice and delights in mercy, who lifted up the widow, the outcast, and the poor, and who exposed hearts of flint everywhere He went. But He does more than repeat the call; He meets our failure to keep it. By His death and resurrection He inaugurates the new covenant in which God takes out the heart of stone and gives a heart of flesh, writing His law within by the Spirit. So the justice and mercy God always desired, and which no hard heart could produce, become possible at last in those united to Christ.
study.god: The God of this chapter looks past the ritual to the heart. He is not impressed by seventy years of fasting if the fasting was really for the people themselves, and He plainly says so. He is passionately concerned with justice, especially for those least able to defend themselves, the widow, the orphan, the foreigner, and the poor, and He calls His people to the loyal covenant mercy that mirrors His own. He is patient, sending prophet after prophet by His Spirit, but He is not mocked; hearts of flint that refuse His word bring real and terrible judgment, a scattering whirlwind that turns a pleasant land into a desolation. And yet the very form of His rebuke, a searching question, is an invitation, opening the door for the returned people to become the just and merciful community He always intended.

crossReferences.note: God rejects fasting joined to injustice and defines the fast He chooses as loosing chains and feeding the hungry.
The LORD requires justice, mercy, and humble walking rather than mere sacrifice, summarizing this call.
Jesus names justice, mercy, and faithfulness the weightier matters of the law, echoing Zechariah's rebuke.
God promises to replace the heart of stone with a heart of flesh, answering the flint hearts of verse 12.

study.oneStory: A question about fasting becomes a summons to the heart of true religion: justice and mercy from a soft heart. The hard hearts that spurned the prophets brought the exile, and the call reaches forward to Christ, who upholds mercy over sacrifice and, by the Spirit of the new covenant, gives hearts of flesh. The story moves toward a people who at last live the justice and mercy God has always desired.

quiz.q: How does God answer the question about whether to keep fasting?

options.text: He asks whether their fasting was ever truly for Him or only for themselves.
options.note: God probes the motive of the heart behind the ritual.

options.text: He gives a simple yes, keep fasting forever.
options.note: He redirects the question to the heart rather than giving a bare yes.

options.text: He forbids all fasting from now on.
options.note: He questions their motive, not the practice itself; chapter 8 turns fasts to feasts.

options.text: He refuses to answer at all.
options.note: He answers fully, redirecting them to justice and mercy.

quiz.q: What does God say He truly desires?

options.text: True justice, loving devotion, and compassion to one another.
options.note: Verses 9 and 10 summarize the message of the earlier prophets.

options.text: More frequent and longer fasts.
options.note: He wants justice and mercy, not increased ritual.

options.text: Larger sacrifices at the temple.
options.note: He prizes mercy over sacrifice, not bigger offerings.

options.text: Complete withdrawal from public life.
options.note: He calls for active justice and care for the vulnerable.

quiz.q: Whom does God specifically say not to oppress?

options.text: The widow, the fatherless, the foreigner, and the poor.
options.note: Verse 10 names the vulnerable God defends.

options.text: Only wealthy landowners.
options.note: His concern is the vulnerable, not the powerful.

options.text: Foreign kings and their armies.
options.note: The focus is the weak within the community, not foreign rulers.

options.text: No one in particular.
options.note: He names four vulnerable groups specifically.

quiz.q: Why did God scatter the earlier generation?

options.text: They made their hearts like flint and refused the prophets' call to justice.
options.note: Their hard-hearted refusal brought the exile, not neglected fasting.

options.text: They fasted too often.
options.note: The problem was refused justice, not excessive fasting.

options.text: They built the temple too quickly.
options.note: The issue was moral, a refusal to do justice and show mercy.

options.text: For no reason God gives.
options.note: God clearly names their hardness of heart as the cause.

quiz.q: How does this call point to Christ?

options.text: Jesus upholds mercy over sacrifice and gives new hearts by the Spirit to do justice.
options.note: He quotes I desire mercy and gives a heart of flesh in the new covenant.

options.text: Jesus abolished justice and mercy as outdated.
options.note: The opposite: He upholds them as the weightier matters of the law.

options.text: Jesus taught that ritual is all that matters.
options.note: He taught mercy and justice over empty ritual.

options.text: Jesus has no link to this passage.
options.note: He stands directly in this prophetic tradition.

questions: God asked whether their fasting was for Him or for themselves. Where might your own religious habits be quietly serving you rather than God?
God links true worship to justice for the widow, orphan, foreigner, and poor. Who are the vulnerable ones near you that God is calling you to defend?
The ancestors made their hearts like flint. Where do you sense your own heart hardening against something God has clearly said?
God desires loving devotion and compassion, not just correct ritual. How could you show warm-hearted mercy to someone this week rather than mere duty?
Christ gives a new heart of flesh by His Spirit. Where do you need Him to soften and renew your heart so that justice and mercy flow naturally?

translationNotes.note: The fifth-month fast mourned the burning of the temple; the seventh-month fast in verse 5 mourned the murder of Gedaliah, both events of Jerusalem's fall.

Loving devotion renders chesed, whose range includes love, goodness, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and covenant loyalty; justice renders mishpat, the right ordering of society that protects the weak.
Stopped up their ears is literally they made their ears too heavy to hear, a vivid image of willful deafness paired with hearts of flint in verse 12.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 7:1-14. All 14 verses reproduced in order. No em or en dashes present in this passage.
